trade.summary: The UAE supplies crude oil and LNG to South Korea, one of the world's largest energy importers. South Korea exports ships, automobiles, electronics, steel, and construction services. Korean construction companies (Samsung, Hyundai) have won massive UAE infrastructure contracts including the Barakah nuclear plant.

diplomatic.summary: The UAE-South Korea Special Strategic Partnership reflects deep ties anchored in the Barakah nuclear plant project (the Arab world's first nuclear power station, built by KEPCO). Korean companies have been major contractors in UAE infrastructure for decades. High-level exchanges are regular and frequent.

military.summary: South Korea deploys the Akh Unit — a special forces unit — to the UAE for training UAE special operations forces, a unique arrangement in Korean military diplomacy. Korea has sold military equipment including K9 howitzers and surveillance systems to the UAE. Defense ties are unusually strong for a non-NATO partner.

financial.summary: Korea National Oil Corporation (KNOC) holds equity stakes in UAE upstream fields. Korean banks have UAE operations. UAE sovereign funds have invested in Korean equities and bonds. The Barakah nuclear project (approx. $25B) represents one of the largest bilateral economic engagements.

energy.summary: South Korea imports approximately 12% of its crude oil from the UAE. The Barakah nuclear plant (4 APR-1400 reactors, full capacity ~5.6GW) is a landmark project. KEPCO signed a 60-year operating agreement. LNG imports from UAE are growing as Korea shifts its energy mix.

scientific.summary: Strong collaboration in nuclear technology, construction engineering, and advanced manufacturing. KEPCO's nuclear technology transfer to UAE forms the basis of an unprecedented scientific partnership. Korean universities have partnerships with UAE institutions in engineering and technology.
